title: Programming Literacy & Training for Life
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

suggested.title: [Book] Science and Practice of Strength Training
suggested.author: Vladimir Zatsiorsky and William Kraemer

supplementary.title: [Book] Periodization: Theory and Methodology of Training
supplementary.author: Tudor Bompa

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: Barbell Medicine, Juggernaut Training Systems

competence_markers.description: The student can design a multi-week training program with appropriate periodization, volume, intensity, and deload
The student can explain the SRA cycle and apply it to their own training decisions
The student meets or is actively approaching the strength standards (deadlift 1.5x BW, squat 1.25x BW, bench 1x BW, 10+ pull-ups)
The student can ruck 10+ miles with appropriate load
The student can sustain physical output for 2+ hours (ruck march, work session, or combination)
The student can coach a beginner through a squat and deadlift with effective cues
The student can articulate why strength training in youth is the highest-return investment in lifelong physical function
The student trains independently — designs, executes, logs, and adjusts their own programming without external direction

cross_connections.description: Movement programming and strength programming integrate at this tier into a unified physical practice.
Advanced nutrition — periodizing intake with training, understanding supplement evidence, managing body composition — directly supports training goals.
Self-directed training without a coach is IC2 at its most demanding. Nobody makes you train. Nobody checks your log. The discipline is internal.
Coaching others is leadership. The ability to teach complex physical skills with patience and clarity transfers directly to every other teaching and leadership context.

content.why_this_matters:
You can lift. You have a training base. You understand progressive overload and energy systems. Now you need to learn to think like a programmer — not following someone else's plan, but designing your own based on your goals, your recovery capacity, your available equipment, and your understanding of how training adaptation actually works.

You also need to think in decades, not weeks. The training decisions you make now — how much you load your joints, whether you respect deload weeks, whether you maintain mobility alongside strength — determine your physical trajectory for the next 40 years. A person who trains intelligently at 16 can be strong and capable at 60. A person who trains recklessly at 16 can be broken at 35. The difference is not talent. It is knowledge applied with discipline.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Program design** — the ability to design training programs based on goals, equipment, and recovery capacity
2. **Periodization understanding** — linear, undulating, and block periodization models
3. **Barbell competence at meaningful standards** — deadlift 1.5–2x BW, squat 1.25–1.75x BW, bench 1–1.5x BW, 10–15 strict pull-ups
4. **Endurance capacity** — ruck 10+ miles with load, sustained physical output for hours
5. **Training science literacy** — supercompensation, stress-recovery-adaptation, overtraining vs. underrecovery
6. **Strength across the lifespan** — understanding why training now serves the body at 60
7. **Coaching basics** — the ability to teach fundamental lifts to a beginner
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Program Design Principles

A training program is not a random collection of exercises. It is a structured plan that manipulates training variables — volume, intensity, frequency, exercise selection, and rest — to produce a specific adaptation over a defined time period.

**Volume:** Total work done. Sets x reps x weight. More volume generally produces more adaptation, up to a point. Beyond that point, more volume produces fatigue without additional benefit.

**Intensity:** How heavy the weight is relative to your maximum. A set of 3 at 90% of your max is high intensity. A set of 12 at 65% is moderate intensity. Intensity determines what quality you are developing — maximal strength at high intensity, hypertrophy and muscular endurance at moderate intensity.

**Frequency:** How often you train a movement or muscle group per week. Most people benefit from training each major movement pattern 2–3 times per week.

**Exercise selection:** Choose compound movements (squat, deadlift, press, row, pull-up) as the foundation. Add accessory work (curls, lateral raises, ab work) to address weaknesses or imbalances. The accessories serve the compounds, not the other way around.

**Rest:** Between sets (1–5 minutes depending on intensity), between sessions (24–72 hours depending on volume), and programmed deload weeks (every 4–6 weeks, reduce volume by 40–60%).

### Part 2: Periodization Models

**Linear periodization:** Start with higher volume and lower intensity. Over weeks, decrease volume and increase intensity. Classic example: Week 1–4: sets of 10. Week 5–8: sets of 8. Week 9–12: sets of 5. Week 13–16: sets of 3. Simple, effective, best for intermediate lifters.

**Undulating periodization:** Vary volume and intensity within the week. Monday: heavy (sets of 3). Wednesday: moderate (sets of 8). Friday: light (sets of 12). Provides variety within each week. Works well for lifters who respond to frequent variation.

**Block periodization:** Dedicate 3–6 week blocks to specific goals. Block 1: Hypertrophy (high volume, moderate intensity). Block 2: Strength (moderate volume, high intensity). Block 3: Peaking (low volume, very high intensity). Each block builds on the previous. Used by competitive athletes approaching a competition.

**What to use:** For most purposes, linear or undulating periodization with regular deloads is sufficient. Block periodization is for competition preparation. Do not overcomplicate programming — the best program is one you execute consistently.

### Part 3: Strength Standards — What Competence Looks Like

These are not competition standards. They are markers of genuine physical capability — the level of strength that makes you meaningfully useful in the real world.

- **Deadlift:** 1.5–2x bodyweight for a single
- **Squat:** 1.25–1.75x bodyweight for a single
- **Bench press:** 1–1.5x bodyweight for a single
- **Overhead press:** 0.65–1x bodyweight for a single
- **Strict pull-ups:** 10–15 reps
- **Run:** Sub-7:00 mile (male) / sub-8:00 (female)
- **Ruck:** 10 miles with 35–50 lb load
- **Carry:** Bodyweight for 200 yards (farmer's walk with total load equal to BW)

Reaching these standards with good form means you are stronger and more capable than the vast majority of the adult population. Maintaining them across decades means you are physically sovereign — your body does what you need it to do, when you need it to.

### Part 4: The Science of Adaptation

**Supercompensation:** Training creates stress. Recovery from that stress produces an adaptation — you become slightly stronger, slightly more conditioned. This is supercompensation. The key insight: the adaptation happens during recovery, not during training. Training without adequate recovery is just damage without rebuilding.

**The stress-recovery-adaptation (SRA) cycle:** Stress (training) → recovery (rest, sleep, nutrition) → adaptation (increased capacity) → new baseline. Each cycle takes 48–72 hours for beginners, longer for advanced trainees. Program training to match this cycle.

**Overtraining vs. underrecovery:** Most people who think they are "overtrained" are actually under-recovered — sleeping poorly, eating inadequately, or dealing with life stress that interferes with physical recovery. True overtraining is rare and requires weeks of excessive volume. Underrecovery is common and fixable. When progress stalls, check sleep, nutrition, and stress before reducing training.

### Part 5: Strength Across the Lifespan

Muscle mass and bone density peak in your mid-20s. After that, without intervention, both decline — slowly at first, then accelerating. By 70, an untrained person may have lost 30–40% of their peak muscle mass. This loss — sarcopenia — is the primary driver of frailty, falls, and loss of independence in old age.

Strength training is the single most effective intervention against this decline. A person who builds substantial strength in their teens and twenties and maintains it through their thirties, forties, and beyond enters old age with physical reserves that an untrained person never had. The 70-year-old who has trained for decades can still carry groceries, climb stairs, get off the floor, and live independently. The 70-year-old who did not may not be able to do any of these things.

**The implication:** Training is not a phase. It is a practice that serves you for your entire life. What you are building now is not just fitness for next season — it is the physical foundation for 50+ years of capability.

### Part 6: Training Others

The ability to teach the basic lifts safely is a marker of genuine understanding. If you can coach a beginner through a squat — cueing the setup, identifying the primary fault, providing one correction that improves the movement — you understand the lift at a level that goes beyond performing it yourself.

**Coaching priorities for a new lifter:**
1. Safety — rack setup, collar placement, spotter positioning, bail-out procedures
2. Position — correct stance, grip, bar position for each lift
3. Bracing — the Valsalva maneuver (big breath, tight core) before every rep
4. Depth/range — full range of motion before any weight is added
5. One cue at a time — identify the worst fault, cue it, see if it fixes secondary faults

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Program Design
**Developing well:** The 12-week program is logical, progressive, includes deloads, balances movement patterns, and accounts for recovery. The student can explain every programming decision.

**Developing slowly:** Program is random or copied from the internet without understanding. Cannot explain choices. No deload. No progression logic.

### 2. Strength Standards
**Developing well:** Meeting or approaching the standards with good form. Training consistently toward gaps.

**Developing slowly:** Stagnated. Not addressing weaknesses. Comparing to social media rather than the actual standards.

### 3. Coaching Ability
**Developing well:** Teaches safely and effectively. Cues are simple and produce improvement. Patient with beginners.

**Developing slowly:** Over-explains. Misses safety considerations. Frustrated by beginner inability.

### 4. Independence
**Developing well:** Trains without external motivation. Logs consistently. Adjusts programming based on results. Seeks improvement.

**Developing slowly:** Needs accountability to train. Inconsistent logging. Does not adjust when something is not working.
